12: zōn gar ho logos tou theou kai energēs kai tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomos kai diiknoumenos achri merismou psychēs kai pneumatos, harmōn te kai myelōn, kai kritikos enthumsōeōn kai ennoiōn kardias — "for the word of God is living and active, sharper than any two-edged sword, piercing to the division of soul and of spirit, of joints and of marrow, and discerning the thoughts and intentions of the heart": zōn (living — present active participle: the word has continuous vitality, not static inscription); energēs (active, effective, at work — same root as energeō); tomōteros hyper pasan machairan distomos (sharper than every two-edged sword — tomōteros: comparative of tomos, sharp): the word's penetrating power exceeds the finest blade. Merismou psychēs kai pneumatos (division of soul and spirit) — not a technical trichotomy but the penetration of the innermost human reality; psychē and pneuma as the two terms for the inner life. Kritikos (discerning, able to judge — the root of krisis): the word not only penetrates but evaluates what it finds.
13: kai ouk estin ktisis aphanēs enōpion autou, panta de gymna kai tetrachēlismena tois ophthalmois autou, pros hon hēmin ho logos — "and no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account" (gymna kai tetrachēlismena: naked and exposed/laid bare — tetrachēlismenā is a NT hapax from trachēlos = neck; the image may be of an animal with its neck bent back for slaughter, fully exposed; or a wrestler pinned with head forced back): the divine omniscience before which all creatures are transparent is the basis of the word's discerning power.
15: ou gar echomen archierea mē dynamenon sympathēsai tais astheneiais hēmōn, pepeirasmenon de kata panta kath' homoiotēta chōris hamartias — "for we do not have a high priest who is unable to sympathize with our weaknesses, but one who in every respect has been tempted as we are, yet without sin" (sympathēsai: to suffer-with, to feel-with — syn + pathein; not merely intellectual comprehension but shared experience): pepeirasmenon (having been tested/tempted — perfect passive participle: permanently in the state of having been tested — the testing is a completed historical event with permanent relevance): kath' homoiotēta (according to likeness, in the same way): the testing was genuinely analogous to human experience. Chōris hamartias (apart from sin): the crucial qualification — tested in every way but without sinning; not "unable to sin" as an ontological constraint but "having withstood every test" as a historical fact.
1: phobēthōmen oun mēpote kataleipoménēs epangelias eiselthein eis tēn katapausin autou dokē tis ex hymōn hysterekenai — "Let us therefore be on guard, lest any of you should seem to have fallen short of the promise of entering his rest" (phobēthōmen: aorist passive subjunctive of phobeomai: to be afraid — the aorist hortatory subjunctive calls for a decisive posture of fear/seriousness): kataleipoménēs epangelias (a promise remaining, a promise still open — genitive absolute): the promise has not been voided by Israel's failure; it still stands available. Dokē hysterekenai (seem to have come short — hystereomai: to fall behind, to lack, to miss the mark): the "seem" is not dismissive but reflects the visible, external evidence of falling short.
2: kai gar esmen euēngelismenoi kathaper kakeinoi, all' ouk ōphelēsen ho logos tēs akoēs ekeinous, mē synkekeramenous tē pistei tois akousasin — "For we have had the good news proclaimed to us just as they did; but the message they heard was of no value to them, because it was not combined with faith in those who heard it" (euēngelismenoi: perfect passive participle of euangelizō: to proclaim good news — the good news has been proclaimed and stands proclaimed over the community): synkekeramenous tē pistei (mixed/combined with faith — perfect passive participle of synkerannymi: to mix together, to blend; the hearing of the word requires faith-blending to produce nourishment): the metaphor may be dietary — food that is not digested and assimilated provides no benefit. Word without faith fails to profit.
3: eiserchometha gar eis [tēn] katapausin hoi pisteusantes — "Now we who have believed are entering that rest" (eiserchometha: present indicative — we are entering, an ongoing process for the believing community): the present tense is significant: entering rest is not entirely future but a present experience of those who believe, alongside its future consummation. Kathōs eirēken, Hōs ōmosa en tē orgē mou, Ei eiseleusontai eis tēn katapausin mou (as he has said: As I swore in my anger, They shall never enter my rest): the citation of the oath is now set against the positive declaration — the excluded generation's oath-bound exclusion is the background against which the believing community's entry stands out.
4: eirēken gar pou peri tēs hebdomēs houtōs, Kai katepausen ho theos en tē hēmera tē hebdomē apo pantōn tōn ergōn autou — "Somewhere in Scripture God spoke about the seventh day in these words: And God rested on the seventh day from all his works" (Gen 2:2 LXX): the indefinite pou (somewhere) is the same distancing device as in 2:6 — Hebrews cites the divine authority, not the human author. Katepausen (rested — aorist of katapauō: to cause to cease, to rest; the same root as katapausis [rest] throughout): God's own Sabbath rest is the archetype of the rest still on offer. The Gen 2:2 citation imports the cosmic Sabbath into the argument: God's rest is not a past event but an ongoing condition into which the believing may enter.
5: kai en toutō palin, Ei eiseleusontai eis tēn katapausin mou — "And again in this passage: They shall never enter my rest" (Ps 95:11 repeated): the second citation of the oath, placed immediately after the Gen 2:2 citation, creates the theological juxtaposition: God's rest (Gen 2) has existed since creation; the oath (Ps 95) shows it was not entered by one generation; therefore it still awaits. The brevity of the re-citation signals that the argument has reached its pivot: the two texts together prove that the rest is real, open, and not yet universally possessed.
6: epei oun apoleipetai tinas eiselthein eis autēn, kai hoi proteron euangelisthentes ouk eisēlthan di' apeithian — "Therefore it still remains for some to enter that rest, and since those who formerly had the good news proclaimed to them did not enter because of disobedience" (apoleipetai: remains, is left over — present passive; the rest is not exhausted but available): tinas eiselthein (for some to enter) — the indefinite tinas (some, certain ones) marks the future entry as possible but not universal. Di' apeithian (because of disobedience — the same root as in 3:18): disobedience is the cause of exclusion; its opposite (obedience of faith) is the condition of entry.
7: palin tina horizei hēmeran, Sēmeron, en Dauid legōn meta tosouton chronon — "God again set a certain day — calling it Today — saying through David much later" (horizei: he sets/defines — from horizō: to mark off a boundary, to determine; the same root as "horizon"): the argument from chronology is precise: David wrote centuries after the exodus generation; the fact that God still speaks the "today" through David proves the rest was not definitively foreclosed in the wilderness but remained open across generations. Sēmeron (Today) — the temporal marker is the key: God keeps reopening the window by repeating the call, which proves the entry remains possible.
8: ei gar autous Iēsous katepausen, ouk an peri allēs elalei meta tauta hēmeras — "For if Joshua had given them rest, God would not have spoken later about another day" (Iēsous: the Greek form of both Joshua and Jesus — a deliberate ambiguity exploited in the argument): the argument is a second-class conditional (contrary to fact): the "if" implies Joshua's giving of rest did not fulfill the promise. The entry into Canaan was a partial, typological rest — not the ultimate katapausis. The fact that God spoke the "today" through David after Joshua's era proves the Canaan-rest was not the final fulfillment.
9: ara apoleipetai sabbatismos tō laō tou theou — "There remains, then, a sabbath-rest for the people of God" (sabbatismos: Sabbath-rest — G4520, a NT hapax; distinct from katapausis used throughout the argument): the change of noun is significant — sabbatismos (Sabbath-keeping, Sabbath-rest) carries the full weight of the weekly Sabbath pattern as a type of the eschatological rest. The Sabbath is not abolished but fulfilled in the rest that remains. Tō laō tou theou (for the people of God): the scope is corporate — the rest belongs to the covenant community, not merely individual souls.
10: ho gar eiselthōn eis tēn katapausin autou kai autos katepausen apo tōn ergōn autou, hōsper apo tōn idiōn ho theos — "For anyone who enters God's rest also rests from their own work, just as God rested from his" (katepausen apo tōn ergōn: rested from the works — the same verb and idiom as Gen 2:2 in v4): the logic is typological: God's Sabbath rest (Gen 2) is the pattern; the believer's entry into rest follows the same pattern of ceasing from work. Apo tōn idiōn (from his own works): the idiōn specifies that the rest is from one's own efforts — faith is the opposite of self-effort as the means of entering God's presence.
11: spoudasōmen oun eiselthein eis ekeinēn tēn katapausin, hina mē en tō autō tis hypodeigmati pesē tēs apeithias — "Let us make every effort to enter that rest, so that no one will fall by following the same pattern of disobedience" (spoudasōmen: aorist hortatory subjunctive of spoudazō: to hasten, to be zealous, to exert oneself — urgency): the paradox of v11 is noted: one must strive (spoudasōmen) to enter a rest. The effort is not the rest itself but the faith-engagement required to move toward it. Hypodeigmati tēs apeithias (the example/pattern of disobedience): hypodeigma (model, pattern, example) — the wilderness generation's failure is not merely a historical story but a structural pattern to be avoided.
14: echontes oun archierea megan dielelythota tous ouranous, Iēsoun ton huion tou theou, kratōmen tēs homologias — "Therefore, since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold firmly to the faith we profess" (dielelythota tous ouranous: perfect active participle of dierchomai: to go through — permanently in the state of having passed through the heavens): the heavenly session is not a static location but the culmination of a journey through the heavens — the Son has traversed the entire cosmic structure and now stands before the Father. Megan archierea (great high priest): the adjective megas marks the superlative quality of this priesthood over all others. Kratōmen tēs homologias (let us hold fast our confession — krateō: to hold firmly, to grip).
16: proserchōmetha oun meta parrēsias tō thronō tēs charitos, hina labōmen eleon kai charin heurōmen eis eukairon boētheian — "Let us then approach God's throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help us in our time of need" (parrēsia: boldness, confidence, freedom of speech — G3954; the right to speak freely before a superior without fear): tō thronō tēs charitos (to the throne of grace): the heavenly throne room, which is the place of judgment in Jewish apocalyptic, is recharacterized by the high priest's presence there as a charis-dispensing court. Eukairon boētheian (timely help, well-timed help — eukairos: opportune, seasonable): the help is not generic but suited to the moment of need.
